Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: in the case of a corporation, to a fine of not more than $1,000,000.

Section.Text: The heading before section 16 of the Act is replaced by the following:

Subsection.Text: The portion of subsection 16(1) of the Act before paragraph (b) is replaced by the following:

If the Minister has reasonable grounds to believe that a vessel is a threat, or poses a direct or indirect risk to the marine ecosystem, the well-being of coastal communities, the security of marine transportation, including to any person, goods, vessel or marine facility, or to the health of persons involved in the marine transportation system, the Minister may direct the vessel

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: to proceed to a place specified by the Minister in accordance with any instructions the Minister may give regarding the route and manner of proceeding and to remain at the place until the Minister is satisfied that the threat or risk no longer exists;

Paragraph.Label: (a.1)
Paragraph.Text: to remain outside of the area described in the schedule or outside of any other area specified by the Minister, except in cases in which the Minister determines no other safe option exists;

Subsection.Text: Section 16 of the Act is amended by adding the following after subsection (1):

If a vessel has been anchored in the area described in the schedule for more than 14 days, the Minister shall direct the vessel to proceed, in accordance with any instructions the Minister may give regarding the route and manner of proceeding, to a place specified by the Minister that is outside of the area and to remain outside of the area for a period specified by the Minister.
Subsections 16(3) and (4) of the Act are replaced by the following:
Every vessel subject to a direction, and its operator, must comply with it.
For greater certainty, a direction issued under this section is not a statutory instrument as defined in subsection 2(1) of the Statutory Instruments Act.

Section.Text: Section 17 of the Act is replaced by the following:

Section.MarginalNote: Offence relating to directions â operator

Subsection.Text: An operator of a vessel that contravenes subsection 16(3) is guilty of an offence and liable

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: on conviction on indictment

Subparagraph.Label: (i)
Subparagraph.Text: in the case of an individual, to a fine of not more than $1,000,000 or to imprisonment for a term of not more than five years, or to both, or

Subparagraph.Label: (ii)
Subparagraph.Text: in the case of a corporation, to a fine of not more than $2,000,000; or

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: on summary conviction

Subparagraph.Label: (i)
Subparagraph.Text: in the case of an individual, to a fine of not more than $500,000 or to imprisonment for a term of not more than two years less a day, or to both, or

Subparagraph.Label: (ii)
Subparagraph.Text: in the case of a corporation, to a fine of not more than $1,000,000.

Subsection.MarginalNote: Offence relating to directions â vessel
Subsection.Text: A vessel that contravenes subsection 16(3) is guilty of an offence and liable on summary conviction to a fine of not more than $1,000,000.

An operator or vessel must not be found to have contravened subsection 16(3) unless it is proved that, at the time of the alleged contravention, reasonable steps had been taken to bring the substance of the direction to the attention of the operator or vessel.

A certificate appearing to be signed by the Minister, stating that a notice containing the direction was given to the operator of the vessel, is evidence that reasonable steps were taken to bring its substance to the attention of the operator.

A certificate appearing to be signed by the Minister, stating that a notice containing the direction was given to the master or to any person on board who is, or appears to be, in command or charge of the vessel, other than the pilot, is evidence that reasonable steps were taken to bring its substance to the attention of the vessel.

If a vessel has been directed to proceed to a port or a marine facility under paragraph 16(1)(a), the Minister may direct the port authority or the person in charge of that port or marine facility to allow that vessel to proceed to, or to moor, anchor or remain at, the port or marine facility.
Every port authority and person subject to a direction issued under subsection (1) must comply with it.
For greater certainty, a direction issued under subsection (1) is not a statutory instrument as defined in subsection 2(1) of the Statutory Instruments Act.
A port authority or a person in charge of a port or a marine facility that contravenes subsection 17.1(2) is guilty of an offence and liable on summary conviction

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: in the case of an individual, to a fine of not more than $500,000 or to imprisonment for a term of not more than two years less a day, or to both; or

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: in the case of a corporation, to a fine of not more than $1,000,000.

Subsection.Text: A port authority or a person in charge of a port or a marine facility must not be found to have contravened subsection 17.1(2), unless it is proved that, at the time of the alleged contravention, reasonable steps had been taken to bring the substance of the direction to the attention of the port authority or person.

A certificate appearing to be signed by the Minister, stating that a notice containing the direction was given to the port authority or the person in charge of a port or a marine facility, is evidence that reasonable steps were taken to bring its substance to the attention of the port authority or person.

Section.Text: The following definitions apply in sections 17.1 and 17.2.

Definition.Text: portâhas the same meaning as in section 5 of the Canada Marine Act.â(port)
port authorityâhas the same meaning as in subsection 2(1) of the Canada Marine Act.â(administration portuaire)

Subsection.Text: If the Minister is of the opinion that there is an immediate threat to the security of marine transportation, including to any person, goods, vessel or marine facility or to the health of persons involved in the marine transportation system, the Minister may direct that any person or vessel do, or refrain from doing, anything that in the opinion of the Minister is necessary to do or refrain from doing in order to respond to the threat, including directions respecting

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: the evacuation of vessels and marine facilities, or portions of them;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: the suspension of access to vessels and marine facilities, or portions of them;

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: the requirement for vessels to proceed to alternate mooring or anchorage sites;

Paragraph.Label: (d)
Paragraph.Text: the movement of persons on vessels or within marine facilities;

Paragraph.Label: (e)
Paragraph.Text: the carrying out of authorized screening;

Paragraph.Label: (f)
Paragraph.Text: the establishment of restricted areas or exclusion zones for vessels; and

Paragraph.Label: (g)
Paragraph.Text: the suspension of marine operations, including operations related to cargo, shipsâ stores and bunkering.

Subsection.Text: An emergency direction comes into force immediately when it is made but ceases to have force 72 hours after it is made, unless the Minister repeals it before the expiry of the 72 hours.
An emergency direction may provide that it applies in lieu of or in addition to any regulation made under this Act, any interim order, any security measure or any security rule.

If there is a conflict between a regulation made under this Act, an interim order, a security measure or a security rule and an emergency direction, the emergency direction prevails to the extent of the conflict.
Every person or vessel subject to an emergency direction must comply with it.
For greater certainty, an emergency direction is not a statutory instrument as defined in subsection 2(1) of the Statutory Instruments Act.

Section.MarginalNote: Offence relating to emergency directions â person

Subsection.Text: Any person that contravenes subsection 17.4(5) is guilty of an offence and liable

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: on conviction on indictment

Subparagraph.Label: (i)
Subparagraph.Text: in the case of an individual, to a fine of not more than $1,000,000 or to imprisonment for a term of not more than five years, or to both, or

Subparagraph.Label: (ii)
Subparagraph.Text: in the case of a corporation, to a fine of not more than $2,000,000; or

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: on summary conviction

Subparagraph.Label: (i)
Subparagraph.Text: in the case of an individual, to a fine of not more than $500,000 or to imprisonment for a term of not more than two years less a day, or to both, or

Subparagraph.Label: (ii)
Subparagraph.Text: in the case of a corporation, to a fine of not more than $1,000,000.

Subsection.MarginalNote: Offence relating to emergency directions â vessel
Subsection.Text: A vessel that contravenes subsection 17.4(5) is guilty of an offence and liable on summary conviction to a fine of not more than $1,000,000.

A person or a vessel must not be found to have contravened subsection 17.4(5) unless it is proved that, at the time of the alleged contravention, the person or vessel had been notified of the emergency direction or reasonable steps had been taken to bring its substance to the notice of those persons or vessels likely to be affected by it.

A certificate appearing to be signed by the Minister, stating that a notice containing the emergency direction was given to persons likely to be affected by it, is evidence that reasonable steps were taken to bring its substance to the attention of those persons.

A certificate appearing to be signed by the Minister, stating that a notice containing the emergency direction was given to the master or to any person on board who is, or appears to be, in command or charge of the vessel, other than the pilot, is evidence that reasonable steps were taken to bring its substance to the attention of the vessel.

Section.Text: Subsections 19.2(2) and (3) of the Act are replaced by the following:

Subsection.MarginalNote: Suspension or cancellation of designation for contraventions
Subsection.Text: The Minister may suspend or cancel the designation of a person as a screening officer if the Minister is of the opinion that the person has contravened this Act or any regulation, interim order, security measure, security rule or emergency direction, or any condition referred to in subsection 10(6).

The Minister may suspend the designation of a person as a screening officer if the Minister is of the opinion that the exercise by the person of the functions of a screening officer may pose a threat or a direct or indirect risk to the security of marine transportation.

Section.Text: Subsection 19.8(1) of the Act is replaced by the following:

Subsection.Text: In the case of a decision made under subsection 19.2(3), if the appeal panel has, on an appeal under section 19.6, dismissed the appeal or if the Minister has, after reconsidering the matter under paragraph 19.5(4)(a) or 19.6(3)(a), confirmed the suspension, the person affected by the decision may, in writing, request the Minister to reconsider whether the exercise by the person of the functions of a screening officer continues to pose, or is likely to continue to pose, a threat or a direct or indirect risk to the security of marine transportation.
Subsection 20(4) of the Act is replaced by the following:

Every person who knowingly makes any false or misleading statement to a screening officer, or knowingly provides false or misleading information to a screening officer, is guilty of an offence punishable on summary conviction and liable to a fine of not more than $500,000 or to imprisonment for a term of not more than two years less a day, or to both.
Paragraphs 20(5)(a) and (b) of the Act are replaced by the following:

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: on conviction on indictment, to a fine of not more than $1,000,000 or to imprisonment for a term of not more than five years, or to both; or

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: on summary conviction, to a fine not exceeding $500,000 or to imprisonment for a term of not more than two years less a day, or to both.

Section.Text: Paragraphs 21(3)(a) and (b) of the Act are replaced by the following:

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: in the case of an individual, to a fine not exceeding $100,000; or

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: in the case of a corporation, to a fine not exceeding $1,000,000.

Subsection.Text: Subsection 23(1) of the Act is replaced by the following:

Section.MarginalNote: Inspection of vessels and marine facilities

Subsection.Text: For the purpose of ensuring compliance with this Act and any regulation, interim order, security measure, security rule or emergency direction, a security inspector may at any reasonable time, but subject to subsection (3), board and inspect any vessel or enter and inspect any marine facility if the inspector is designated to inspect the vessel or facility.
Paragraph 23(2)(b) of the Act is replaced by the following:

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: require any person to produce for inspection and copying any document that the inspector believes, on reasonable grounds, contains any information relevant to the administration of this Act or the regulation, interim order, security measure, security rule or emergency direction;

Subsection.Text: Subparagraphs 25(4)(a)(i) and (ii) of the Act are replaced by the following:

Subparagraph.Label: (i)
Subparagraph.Text: in the case of an individual, to a fine of not more than $1,000,000 or to imprisonment for a term of not more than five years, or to both, or

Subparagraph.Label: (ii)
Subparagraph.Text: in the case of a corporation, to a fine of not more than $2,000,000; or

Subsection.Text: Subparagraphs 25(4)(b)(i) and (ii) of the Act are replaced by the following:

Subparagraph.Label: (i)
Subparagraph.Text: in the case of an individual, to a fine of not more than $500,000 or to imprisonment for a term of not more than two years less a day, or to both, or

Subparagraph.Label: (ii)
Subparagraph.Text: in the case of a corporation, to a fine of not more than $1,000,000.

Section.Text: Section 26 of the English version of the Act is replaced by the following:

If an offence is committed or continued on more than one day, the person or vessel that committed it is liable to be convicted of a separate offence for each day on which the offence is committed or continued.
Section 28 of the Act is amended by adding the following after subsection (4):

Subsection.Text: Subsections (1) and (4) do not apply in respect of offences under subsection 20(4) and paragraphs 25(3)(a) to (c) and (e).

In a prosecution of a vessel for an offence under this Act, the vessel is liable to be convicted of the offence if it is established that the offence was committed by the operator of the vessel or by any person on board, other than a security inspector, whether or not the person on board has been identified, prosecuted or convicted.

Section.Text: Section 29 of the Act is replaced by the following:

Subsection.Text: A person must not be found guilty of an offence under this Act, other than an offence under subsection 20(4) or paragraph 25(3)(a), (b), (c) or (e), if they establish that they exercised due diligence to prevent its commission.

A vessel must not be found guilty of an offence under this Act if the person who committed the act or omission that constitutes the offence establishes that they exercised due diligence to prevent its commission.

Section.Text: Subsection 31(1) of the Act is replaced by the following:

Subsection.Text: If a fine imposed on a person or vessel convicted of an offence under this Act is not paid when required, the conviction may be registered in the superior court of the province in which the trial was held and, when registered, has the same effect as if the conviction were a judgment of that court obtained by His Majesty in right of Canada against the convicted person or vessel for a debt in the amount of the fine.
The portion of subsection 33(1) of the English version of the Act before subparagraph (a)(ii) is replaced by the following:

Section.MarginalNote: If reasonable grounds to believe a violation

Subsection.Text: If the Minister has reasonable grounds to believe that a person or vessel has committed a violation, the Minister may

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: enter into an assurance of compliance with the person or vessel that

Subparagraph.Label: (i)
Subparagraph.Text: identifies the violation and provides that the person or vessel will comply with the provision to which the violation relates within the period, and be subject to the terms and conditions, specified in the assurance,

Subsection.Text: Subparagraph 33(1)(a)(iii) of the English version of the Act is replaced by the following:

Subparagraph.Label: (iii)
Subparagraph.Text: sets out the penalty, fixed by or within the range fixed by the regulations, that the person or vessel would have been liable to pay for the violation if the assurance had not been entered into; or

Subsection.Text: The portion of paragraph 33(1)(b) of the English version of the Act before subparagraph (ii) is replaced by the following:

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: issue, and cause to be served on the person or vessel, a notice of violation that names the person or vessel, identifies the violation and sets out

Subparagraph.Label: (i)
Subparagraph.Text: the penalty, fixed by or within the range fixed by the regulations, that the person or vessel is liable to pay for the violation,

Subsection.Text: Subsection 33(2) of the English version of the Act is replaced by the following:

The Minister may extend the period specified under subparagraph (1)(a)(i) if the Minister is satisfied that the person or vessel is unable to comply with the assurance of compliance for reasons beyond the person or vesselâs control.

Section.Text: Sections 34 and 35 of the English version of the Act are replaced by the following:

Subsection.Text: A person or vessel that enters into an assurance of compliance under paragraph 33(1)(a) is, unless a review is requested under subsection (2), deemed to have committed the violation in respect of which the assurance was entered into.

A person or vessel that enters into an assurance of compliance may, within 48 hours after the assurance is signed, unless a notice of default is served within that period under subsection 36(1), file a request with the Tribunal for a review of the facts of the violation, in which case the assurance is deemed to be a notice of violation and a review under subsection 39(1) of the facts of the violation and of the amount of the penalty is deemed to have been requested.

Section.MarginalNote: When assurance of compliance complied with
Section.Text: If the Minister is satisfied that an assurance of compliance under paragraph 33(1)(a) has been complied with, the Minister must cause a notice to that effect to be served on the person or vessel and, on the service of the notice,

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: no further proceedings may be taken against the person or vessel with respect to the violation in respect of which the assurance was entered into; and

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: any security deposited under subparagraph 33(1)(a)(ii) must be returned to the person or vessel.

Subsection.Text: The portion of subsection 36(1) of the English version of the Act before paragraph (b) is replaced by the following:

Section.MarginalNote: When assurance of compliance not complied with

Subsection.Text: If the Minister is of the opinion that a person or vessel that has entered into an assurance of compliance has not complied with it, the Minister may cause a notice of default to be served on the person or vessel to the effect that, unless a member determines under section 37, or an appeal panel decides under section 40, that the assurance has been complied with,

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: the person or vessel is liable to pay double the amount of the penalty set out in the assurance; or

Subsection.Text: Subsection 36(3) of the English version of the Act is replaced by the following:
On the service of a notice of default, the person or vessel served has no right of set-off or compensation against any amount spent by the person or vessel under the assurance of compliance.
Subsections 37(1) to (4) of the English version of the Act are replaced by the following:

A person or vessel that is served with a notice under subsection 36(1) may file a written request for a review, on or before the date specified in the notice or within any further time that the Tribunal on application allows.

On receipt of a request filed under subsection (1), the Tribunal must appoint a time and place for the review and must notify the Minister and the person or vessel that filed the request of the time and place in writing.

The member of the Tribunal assigned to conduct the review must provide the Minister and the person or vessel that filed the request with an opportunity consistent with procedural fairness and natural justice to present evidence and make representations.

The burden is on the Minister to establish that the person or vessel did not comply with the assurance of compliance referred to in the notice. The person or vessel is not required, and must not be compelled, to give any evidence or testimony in the matter.
Subsection 37(5) of the Act is replaced by the following:
Despite section 45, a person or vessel does not have a defence by reason that the person or vessel exercised all due diligence to comply with the assurance of compliance.
Subsection 37(6) of the English version of the Act is replaced by the following:
The member may confirm the Ministerâs decision or determine that the person or vessel has complied with the assurance of compliance.

Section.Text: The portion of section 38 of the English version of the Act before paragraph (b) is replaced by the following:
Any security deposited under subparagraph 33(1)(a)(ii) must be returned to the person or vessel if

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: the person or vessel pays double the amount of the penalty set out in the assurance of compliance in accordance with a notice under subsection 36(1); or

Subsection.Text: The portion of subsection 39(1) of the English version of the Act before paragraph (a) is replaced by the following:
A person or vessel served with a notice of violation under paragraph 33(1)(b) must
Subsections 39(2) to (5) of the English version of the Act are replaced by the following:
If a review of the facts of the violation is not requested, the person or vessel is deemed to have committed the violation in respect of which the notice was served.

On receipt of a request filed under paragraph (1)(b), the Tribunal must appoint a time and place for the review and must notify the Minister and the person or vessel that filed the request of the time and place in writing.

The member of the Tribunal assigned to conduct the review must provide the Minister and the person or vessel that filed the request with an opportunity consistent with procedural fairness and natural justice to present evidence and make representations.

The burden is on the Minister to establish that the person or vessel committed the violation referred to in the notice. The person or vessel is not required, and must not be compelled, to give any evidence or testimony in the matter.

Section.Text: Subsection 40(1) of the English version of the Act is replaced by the following:

Subsection.Text: The Minister or the person or vessel that requested the review of the decision may appeal a determination made under subsection 37(6) or 39(6) to the Tribunal within 30 days after the determination.

Section.Text: Section 45 of the Act is replaced by the following:

Subsection.Text: A person must not be found to be liable for a violation if they establish that they exercised due diligence to prevent its commission.

A vessel must not be found to be liable for a violation if the person that committed the act or omission that constitutes the violation establishes that they exercised due diligence to prevent its commission.
Subsection 46(1) of the English version of the Act is replaced by the following:

Section.MarginalNote: Violation by employee or agent or mandatary

Subsection.Text: A person is liable for a violation committed by their employee or agent or mandatary, whether or not the employee or agent or mandatary has been identified or proceeded against under sections 33 to 43.
Subsections 46(2) and (3) of the French version of the Act are replaced by the following:

Lâexploitant dâun bÃ¢timent peut Ãªtre tenu responsable dâune violation, en rapport avec ce bÃ¢timent, commise par une autre personne, que celle-ci ait Ã©tÃ© ou non identifiÃ©e ou poursuivie aux termes des articles 33 Ã 43 de la prÃ©sente loi, Ã moins que, lors de la violation, le bÃ¢timent nâait Ã©tÃ© en la possession dâune autre personne sans son consentement.

Lâexploitant dâune installation maritime peut Ãªtre tenu responsable dâune violation, en rapport avec cette installation maritime, commise par une autre personne avec son consentement, que cette personne ait Ã©tÃ© ou non identifiÃ©e ou poursuivie aux termes des articles 33 Ã 43 de la prÃ©sente loi.
Subsection 46(4) of the English version of the Act is replaced by the following:

An officer, director or agent or mandatary of a corporation that commits a violation under this Act is a party to and liable for the violation if they directed, authorized, assented to, acquiesced in or participated in the commission of the violation, whether or not the corporation has been identified or proceeded against under sections 33 to 43.

Section.Text: The Act is amended by adding the following after section 46:

Subsection.Text: It is sufficient proof that a vessel has committed a violation to establish that the act or omission that constitutes the violation was committed by the operator of the vessel or by any person on board, other than a security inspector, whether or not the person on board has been identified or proceeded against under sections 33 to 43.

Subsection.MarginalNote: Party to violation committed by vessel
Subsection.Text: If a vessel commits a violation under this Act and the operator of the vessel directed, authorized, assented to, acquiesced in or participated in the commission of the violation, the operator is a party to and liable for the violation, whether or not the vessel has been proceeded against under sections 33 to 43.
The portion of subsection 47(1) of the English version of the Act before paragraph (b) is replaced by the following:

Unless the Minister is of the opinion that it is not in the public interest to do so, five years after the last day on which any of the following events occurs, the Minister is to remove every notation of violation or default, and every suspension or cancellation of the designation of or refusal to designate or to renew the designation of a person as a screening officer on prescribed grounds, from any records that the Minister may keep respecting the person or vessel:

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: payment by the person or vessel of every penalty that the person or vessel is liable to pay after a notice of violation, a notice of default, a determination of a member of the Tribunal on review or a decision of an appeal panel of the Tribunal; or

Subsection.Text: Subsection 47(2) of the English version of the Act is replaced by the following:
When the Minister is of the opinion that removal of a notation is not in the public interest, the Minister must give notice of that fact to the person or vessel.
Subsections 47(6) and (7) of the English version of the Act are replaced by the following:
The person or vessel may, within thirty days after a determination is made under subsection (5), appeal the determination to the Tribunal.

If the person or vessel does not appear at the review hearing, the person or vessel is not entitled to appeal a determination unless they establish that there was sufficient reason to justify their absence.

Section.Text: Section 48 of the Act is replaced by the following:
The Minister must keep a public record of notations of violations or default that appear on any record that the Minister may keep respecting persons or vessels under this Act.
Paragraph 51(c) of the Act is replaced by the following:

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: designating violations that may be proceeded with by issuing notices of violation and fixing a penalty or a range of penalties in respect of each such violation, up to a maximum ofÂ $250,000 but in any event not greater than the maximum fine that would be payable if the violation were proceeded with by way of summary conviction;

Paragraph.Label: (c.1)
Paragraph.Text: respecting persons who can request a review on behalf of a vessel in relation to an alleged violation by the vessel;

Paragraph.Label: (c.2)
Paragraph.Text: respecting the service of documents, including the proof of their service and the circumstances under which they are deemed to have been served, on a vessel; and

Section.Text: The Act is amended by adding, after section 51, the schedule set out in the schedule to this Act.
In sections 87 to 90, other Act means the Marine Transportation Security Act, as amended by sections 50 to 85 of this Act.

Subsection.Text: A contravention of subsection 6.1(5) of the other Act is deemed to be a violation for the purposes of sections 33 to 51 of the other Act and the provisions of any regulations made under section 51 of the other Act.
The range of penalties in respect of a violation referred to in subsection (1) is $260 to $250,000.
A violation referred to in subsection (1) constitutes a separate violation for each day on which it is continued.

This section is repealed on the day on which a regulation made under section 51 of the other Act that designates the contravention of subsection 6.1(5) of the other Act as a violation for the purposes of section 32 of the other Act comes into force.

A contravention of subsection 16(3) of the other Act is deemed to be a violation for the purposes of sections 33 to 51 of the other Act and the provisions of any regulations made under section 51 of the other Act.
The range of penalties in respect of a violation referred to in subsection (1) is $2,625 to $250,000.
A violation referred to in subsection (1) constitutes a separate violation for each day on which it is continued.

This section is repealed on the day on which a regulation made under section 51 of the other Act that designates the contravention of subsection 16(3) of the other Act as a violation for the purposes of section 32 of the other Act comes into force.

A contravention of 17.1(2) of the other Act is deemed to be a violation for the purposes of sections 33 to 51 of the other Act and the provisions of any regulations made under section 51 of the other Act.
The range of penalties in respect of a violation referred to in subsection (1) is $1,300 to $250,000.
A violation referred to in subsection (1) constitutes a separate violation for each day on which it is continued.

This section is repealed on the day on which a regulation made under section 51 of the other Act that designates the contravention of subsection 17.1(2) of the other Act as a violation for the purposes of section 32 of the other Act comes into force.

A contravention of subsection 17.4(5) of the other Act is deemed to be a violation for the purposes of sections 33 to 51 of the other Act and the provisions of any regulations made under section 51 of the other Act.
The range of penalties in respect of a violation referred to in subsection (1) is $1,300 to $250,000.
A violation referred to in subsection (1) constitutes a separate violation for each day on which it is continued.

This section is repealed on the day on which a regulation made under section 51 of the other Act that designates the contravention of subsection 17.4(5) of the other Act as a violation for the purposes of section 32 of the other Act comes into force.
In this section, other Act means the Protecting Canadaâs Immigration System Act.
If subsection 53(2) of this Act comes into force before section 70 of the other Act, then that section 70 is repealed.
If section 70 of the other Act comes into force before subsection 53(2) of this Act, then

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: that subsection 53(2) is deemed never to have come into force and is repealed; and

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: on the day on which section 50 of this Act comes into force, subsections 5(2) and (3) of the Marine Transportation Security Act are replaced by the following:

Subsection.MarginalNote: Regulations â security of marine transportation
Subsection.Text: The Governor in Council may make regulations respecting threats or direct or indirect risks to the security of marine transportation including the security of people, goods, vessels and marine facilities and to the health of persons involved in the marine transportation system.
The Governor in Council may make regulations respecting fees and charges to be paid in relation to the administration and enforcement of this Act.
Every person who contravenes a regulation made under subsection (1) or (2) is guilty of an offence and liable

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: on conviction on indictment

Subparagraph.Label: (i)
Subparagraph.Text: in the case of an individual, to a fine of not more than $1,000,000 or to imprisonment for a term of not more than five years, or to both, or

Subparagraph.Label: (ii)
Subparagraph.Text: in the case of a corporation, to a fine of not more than $2,000,000; or

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: on summary conviction

Subparagraph.Label: (i)
Subparagraph.Text: in the case of an individual, to a fine of not more than $500,000 or to imprisonment for a term of not more than two years less a day, or to both, or

Subparagraph.Label: (ii)
Subparagraph.Text: in the case of a corporation, to a fine of not more than $1,000,000.

Subsection.Text: A vessel that contravenes a regulation made under subsection (1) or (2) is guilty of an offence and liable on summary conviction to a fine of not more than $1,000,000.
If section 70 of the other Act comes into force on the same day as subsection 53(2) of this Act, then that section 70 is deemed never to have come into force and is repealed.
If section 54 of this Act comes into force before section 71 of the other Act, then that section 71 is repealed.
If section 71 of the other Act comes into force before section 54 of this Act, then

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: that section 54 is deemed never to have come into force and is repealed; and

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: on the day on which section 50 of this Act comes into force, section 5.1 of the Marine Transportation Security Act is replaced by the following:

Section.Text: The Governor in Council may make regulations respecting the disclosure of information collected for the purposes of this Act by the Minister to

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: departments or agencies of the federal government or members or agents of such departments or agencies; or

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: departments or agencies of the government of a province or agencies of a municipality or members or agents or mandataries of such departments or agencies.

Subsection.Text: If section 71 of the other Act comes into force on the same day as section 54 of this Act, then that section 71 is deemed never to have come into force and is repealed.
If section 62 of this Act comes into force before section 72 of the other Act, then that section 72 is repealed.
If section 72 of the other Act comes into force on the same day as section 62 of this Act, then that section 72 is deemed never to have come into force and is repealed.
If section 68 of this Act comes into force before section 73 of the other Act, then that section 73 is repealed.
If section 73 of the other Act comes into force before section 68 of this Act, then, on the day on which that section 68 comes into force,

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: the portion of paragraph 25(4) of the Marine Transportation Security Act before paragraph (a) is replaced by the following:

Subsection.Text: Every person who contravenes this section is guilty of an offence and liable

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: subsection 25(5) of the Marine Transportation Security Act is repealed.

Subsection.Text: If section 73 of the other Act comes into force on the same day as section 68 of this Act, then that section 73 is deemed never to have come into force and is repealed.
If section 69 of this Act comes into force before section 74 of the other Act, then that section 74 is repealed.
If section 74 of the other Act comes into force before section 69 of this Act, then that section 69 is deemed never to have come into force and is repealed.
If section 74 of the other Act comes into force on the same day as section 69 of this Act, then that section 74 is deemed never to have come into force and is repealed.
If section 70 of this Act comes into force before section 75 of the other Act, then that section 75 is repealed.
If section 75 of the other Act comes into force before section 70 of this Act, then

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: that section 70 is deemed never to have come into force and is repealed; and

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: on the day on which section 50 of this Act comes into force, subsections 28(5) and (6) of the Marine Transportation Security Act are replaced by the following:

Subsection.Text: Subsections (1) and (4) do not apply in respect of offences under subsection 20(4) and paragraphs 25(3)(a) to (c) and (e).

In a prosecution of a vessel for an offence under this Act, the vessel is liable to be convicted of the offence if it is established that the offence was committed by the operator of the vessel or by any person on board, other than a security inspector, whether or not the person on board has been identified, prosecuted or convicted.
If section 75 of the other Act comes into force on the same day as section 70 of this Act, then that section 75 is deemed never to have come into force and is repealed.
If section 71 of this Act comes into force before section 76 of the other Act, then that section 76 is repealed.
If section 76 of the other Act comes into force on the same day as section 71 of this Act, then that section 76 is deemed never to have come into force and is repealed.
If section 72 of this Act comes into force before section 77 of the other Act, then that section 77 is repealed.
If section 77 of the other Act comes into force before section 72 of this Act, then

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: that section 72 is deemed never to have come into force and is repealed;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: on the day on which section 50 of this Act comes into force, subsection 31(1) of the English version of the Marine Transportation Security Act is replaced by the following:

Subsection.Text: If a fine imposed on a person or vessel convicted of an offence under this Act is not paid when required, the conviction may be registered in the superior court of the province in which the trial was held and, when registered, has the same effect as if the conviction were a judgment of that court obtained by His Majesty in right of Canada against the convicted person or vessel for a debt in the amount of the fine.
If section 77 of the other Act comes into force on the same day as section 72 of this Act, then that section 77 is deemed never to have come into force and is repealed.

Section.Text: Section 6.2 of the Canada Transportation Act is amended by adding the following after subsection (3):

Subsection.Text: For greater certainty, an electronic system, including an automated system, may be used by the Minister to make a decision or determination under this Act or any other Act of Parliament that the Minister administers or enforces, and may be used by a designated person to make a decision or determination under any such Act if the system is made available to the designated person by the Minister.

Section.Text: The Act is amended by adding the following after section 6.4:

Subsection.Text: For a purpose related to verifying compliance or preventing non-compliance with an Act of Parliament that the Minister administers or enforces, or regulations made under that Act, or for a purpose related to determining if a violation within the meaning of that Act has been committed, a person is considered to have entered a place when accessing it remotely by a means of telecommunication.

Subsection.MarginalNote: Limitation â place not accessible to the public
Subsection.Text: A person who, by a means of telecommunication, accesses remotely a place that is not accessible to the public must do so with the knowledge of the owner or person in charge of the place and must be remotely in the place for no longer than the period necessary for the purpose referred to in subsection (1).
Subsection 53.1(2) of the Act is replaced by the following:

Subsection.MarginalNote: Notice â transportation undertaking situated in a port
Subsection.Text: A person to whom subsection (1) does not apply and who is a party to a proposed transaction that meets the following conditions must give notice to the Minister and, at the same time, to the Commissioner of Competition of the proposed transaction before it is completed:

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: the transaction is not referred to in any of sections 111, 112 or 113 of the Competition Act;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: it involves a transportation undertaking situated in a port, within the meaning of section 5 of the Canada Marine Act; and

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: the aggregate value of the assets in Canada that are the subject of the transaction, or the gross revenues from sales in or from Canada generated from those assets, exceed $10,000,000, as calculated under any of subsections 110(2) to (6) of the Competition Act, in accordance with the type of proposed transaction.

Subsection.Text: Subsection (1.1) does not apply to

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: an individual who is a natural person within the meaning of paragraph (b) of that definition in Article 8.1 of the Comprehensive Economic and Trade Agreement between Canada and the European Union and its Member States, done at Brussels on October 30, 2016, or in the version of that Article 8.1 as it is incorporated by reference to the Agreement on Trade Continuity between Canada and the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland, done at Ottawa, on December 9, 2020, by that agreementâs Article I; or

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: an enterprise of a Party that is an investor within the meaning of those definitions in Article 8.1 of the Comprehensive Economic and Trade Agreement between Canada and the European Union and its Member States, done at Brussels on October 30, 2016, or in the version of that Article 8.1 as it is incorporated by reference to the Agreement on Trade Continuity between Canada and the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland, done at Ottawa, on December 9, 2020, by that agreementâs Article I â other than such an enterprise that is constituted or organized under Canadian law.

Subsection.Text: A notice given under subsection (1) must, subject to the regulations, contain the information required under subsection 114(1) of the Competition Act. The notice must also contain any information with respect to the public interest as it relates to national transportation that is required under any guidelines that the Minister issues and publishes. After receipt of a notice, the Minister may require the person who has given the notice to provide further information.

A notice given under subsection (1.1) must, subject to the regulations, contain the information that would be required under subsection 114(1) of the Competition Act if the person giving the notice were required to give notice under that subsection. The notice must also contain any information with respect to the public interest as it relates to national transportation that is required under any guidelines that the Minister issues and publishes. After receipt of a notice, the Minister may require the person who has given the notice to provide further information.
Subsections 53.1(2.1) and (3) of the English version of the Act are replaced by the following:

Guidelines referred to in subsections (2) and (2.01) must be elaborated in consultation with the Competition Bureau and must include factors that may be considered to determine whether a proposed transaction raises issues with respect to the public interest as it relates to national transportation.
Guidelines referred to in subsections (2) and (2.01) are not statutory instruments within the meaning of the Statutory Instruments Act.
Subsection 53.1(4) of the Act is replaced by the following:

If the Minister is of the opinion that the proposed transaction does not raise issues with respect to the public interest as it relates to national transportation, the Minister must, within 42 days after a person gives notice under subsection (1) or (1.1), give notice of the opinion to that person, in which case sections 53.2 and 53.3 do not apply in respect of that transaction.
Subsections 53.2(1) and (2) of the Act are replaced by the following:

A person must not complete a proposed transaction referred to in subsection 53.1(1) or (1.1) unless the transaction is approved by the Governor in Council and, in the case of a transaction referred to in section 53.3, the Agency determines that the transaction would result in an air transportation undertaking that is Canadian as defined in subsection 55(1).

The Commissioner of Competition must within 150 days after the Commissioner is notified of a proposed transaction under subsection 114(1) of the Competition Act, or within any longer period that the Minister may allow, report to the Minister and the parties to the transaction on any concerns regarding potential prevention or lessening of competition that may occur as a result of the transaction.

The Commissioner of Competition may within 150 days after the Commissioner is notified of a proposed transaction under subsection 53.1(1.1), or within any longer period that the Minister may allow, report to the Minister and the parties to the transaction on any concerns regarding potential prevention or lessening of competition that may occur as a result of the transaction.
The portion of subsection 53.2(4) of the Act before paragraph (a) is replaced by the following:

Subsection.MarginalNote: Concerns relating to public interest and competition
Subsection.Text: After receipt of the Commissionerâs report, if applicable, and any report given under subsection 53.1(6), but before the Minister makes a recommendation for the purposes of subsection (7), the Minister must

Section.Text: Paragraph 53.5(a) of the Act is replaced by the following:

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: specifying information required in notices under subsections 53.1(1) and (1.1); and

Section.Text: The portion of subsection 53.6(1) of the Act before paragraph (a) is replaced by the following:

Subsection.Text: Every person who contravenes subsection 53.1(1) or (1.1) is guilty of an offence and is liable

Section.Text: The definition arrangement in section 53.7 of the Act is replaced by the following:

Definition.Text: arrangementâmeans an agreement or arrangement, other than a transaction referred to in subsection 53.1(1) or (1.1), involving two or more transportation undertakings providing air services, as defined in subsection 55(1), to, from or within Canada, to coordinate on any aspect of the operation or marketing of such services, including prices, routes, schedules, capacity or ancillary services and to share costs or revenues or other resources or benefits.â(entente)

Section.Text: The Act is amended by adding the following before the heading âEnforcementâ before section 173:

Section.MarginalNote: Works for the general advantage of Canada
Section.Text: The terminals situated within a port, within the meaning of section 5 of the Canada Marine Act, are declared to be works for the general advantage of Canada.
Paragraph 4(f) of the Canada Marine Act is replaced by the following:

Paragraph.Label: (f)
Paragraph.Text: manage the marine infrastructure and services in a commercial manner that encourages, and takes into account, input from users, Indigenous peoples and the community in which a port or harbour is located or in proximity to which vessels are regularly anchored;

Paragraph.Label: (f.1)
Paragraph.Text: manage the marine infrastructure and services, including through the participation of port authorities, in a manner that maintains the security and enhances the resiliency of supply chains, safeguards national security and promotes healthy competition dynamics;

Paragraph.Label: (f.2)
Paragraph.Text: manage traffic, including mooring and anchorage, in order to protect the marine environment, to respect the rights of Indigenous peoples and to promote the efficiency of supply chains as well as the protection of the environment and the well-being of the communities in proximity to which vessels are regularly anchored;

Paragraph.Label: (f.3)
Paragraph.Text: reduce greenhouse gas emissions from the operation of ports;

Paragraph.Label: (f.4)
Paragraph.Text: protect the marine environment;

Subsection.Text: The portion of paragraph 8(2)(f) of the Act before subparagraph (i) is replaced by the following:

Paragraph.Label: (f)
Paragraph.Text: the number of directors, between seven and 13, to be appointed under section 14, to be chosen as follows:

Subsection.Text: Subparagraphs 8(2)(f)(ii) to (iv) of the Act are replaced by the following:

Subparagraph.Label: (ii)
Subparagraph.Text: up to two individuals appointed by the municipalities mentioned in the letters patent,

Subparagraph.Label: (iii)
Subparagraph.Text: in the case of a port wholly or partially located in Vancouver, Prince Rupert or Thunder Bay, one individual appointed by the province in which the port is situated and another individual appointed by the Provinces of Alberta, Saskatchewan and Manitoba acting together, and, in any other case, up to two individuals appointed by the provinces mentioned in the letters patent, and

Subparagraph.Label: (iv)
Subparagraph.Text: the remaining individuals nominated by the Minister in consultation with the users selected by the Minister or the classes of users mentioned in the letters patent, including one individual nominated by the Minister in consultation with the labour groups selected by the Minister or with those mentioned in the letters patent;

Subsection.Text: Subsection 8(2) of the Act is amended by adding the following after paragraph (g):

Paragraph.Label: (g.1)
Paragraph.Text: the principles and guidelines governing the port authorityâs advisory committees established under section 33.1, including their composition and administration;

Subsection.Text: Subsection 8(2) of the Act is amended by adding the following after paragraph (i):

Paragraph.Label: (i.1)
Paragraph.Text: the extent to which the port authority may undertake jointly with other port authorities, through a corporation, partnership, joint venture, association or other entity, the activities referred to in subsection 28(2);

Subsection.Text: Subsection 8(2) of the Act is amended by adding the following after paragraph (j):

Paragraph.Label: (j.1)
Paragraph.Text: the schedule for the development of the port authorityâs land-use plans, setting out intervals of no more than five years;

Subsection.Text: Paragraph 8(2)(l) of the Act is replaced by the following:

Paragraph.Label: (l)
Paragraph.Text: the limits on the power of the port authority to borrow money on the credit of the port authority for port purposes;

Paragraph.Label: (l.1)
Paragraph.Text: the schedule for the submission of the port authorityâs borrowing plans, setting out intervals of no more than three years; and

Subsection.Text: Paragraph 14(1)(b) of the Act is replaced by the following:

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: the municipalities mentioned in the letters patent appoint one or two individuals, as mentioned in the letters patent;

Subsection.Text: Paragraph 14(1)(d) of the Act is replaced by the following:

Paragraph.Label: (d)
Paragraph.Text: the Governor in Council appoints the remaining individuals nominated by the Minister in consultation with users selected by the Minister or the classes of users mentioned in the letters patent, including one individual nominated by the Minister in consultation with the labour groups selected by the Minister or with those mentioned in the letters patent.

Subsection.Text: Section 14 of the Act is amended by adding the following after subsection (1.1):

Subsection.MarginalNote: Directors appointed by board of directors
Subsection.Text: The board of directors of a port authority may appoint a director under paragraph (1)(a) or (d) if the position has been vacant for more than twelve months.
The board of directors of a port authority shall give notice to the Minister of its intention to make an appointment under subsection (1.2) at least 90 days before doing so.
Subsection 14(2) of the Act is replaced by the following:

Directors are appointed to hold office for a renewable term of not more than three years that will ensure as far as possible the expiry in any one year of the terms of office of not more than one half of the directors.

Section.Text: The Act is amended by adding the following after section 15:

Each director of a port authority appointed under paragraph 14(1)(b) or (c) must undergo the security assessment that the Minister determines to be appropriate and maintain the security certification from the Government of Canada that is obtained as a result of that assessment.

Subsection.Text: Paragraphs 16(a) and (b) of the Act are replaced by the following:

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: a mayor or councillor of a municipality mentioned in the letters patent;

Paragraph.Label: (a.1)
Paragraph.Text: an officer or employee of a municipality mentioned in the letters patent whose employment would result in them being in a conflict of interest with the activities of the port authority;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: a member of the legislature of a province mentioned in the letters patent;

Paragraph.Label: (b.1)
Paragraph.Text: an officer or employee of the public service of a province mentioned in the letters patent whose employment would result in them being in a conflict of interest with the activities of the port authority;

Subsection.Text: Paragraph 16(c.1) of the Act is replaced by the following:

Paragraph.Label: (c.1)
Paragraph.Text: an officer or employee of the federal public administration or a port authority;

Section.Text: The Act is amended by adding the following after section 17:

In the event of the absence or incapacity of the chairperson, or a vacancy in that office, the vice-chairperson or, if there is no vice-chairperson, a member of the board who is designated by a resolution of the board of directors is authorized to act as chairperson for a term of no more than 90 days.
Subsection 19(1) of the Act is amended by adding the following after paragraph (b):

Paragraph.Label: (b.1)
Paragraph.Text: no longer has a valid security certification referred to in section 15.1, if the director was appointed under paragraph 14(1)(b) or (c);

Subsection.Text: Subsection 27(1) of the Act is amended by striking out âandâ at the end of paragraph (g) and by adding the following after paragraph (h):

Paragraph.Label: (i)
Paragraph.Text: respecting the administrative requirements for a port authorityâs advisory committees established under section 33.1;

Paragraph.Label: (j)
Paragraph.Text: respecting the conduct of governance assessments carried out under section 33.2 and the information a port authority must provide to the Minister in its report in respect of such an assessment; and

Paragraph.Label: (k)
Paragraph.Text: providing for the information that must be included in a port authorityâs business plan under section 39.

Subsection.Text: Subsections 27(2) and (3) of the Act are replaced by the following:

Subsection.MarginalNote: Regulations â environment and climate change
Subsection.Text: For the purposes of this Part, the Governor in Council may make regulations respecting the impact of the operation of a port by a port authority on the environment, including climate change, and the impact of climate change on the operation of a port, including regulations

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: establishing absolute greenhouse gas emissions reduction targets in respect of the operation of a port by a port authority that are consistent with Canadaâs international commitments and with the national greenhouse gas emissions targets for each milestone year under the Canadian Net-Zero Emissions Accountability Act;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: respecting the contents of a five-year climate change plan;

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: respecting the contents of a five-year plan respecting climate change adaptation actions;

Paragraph.Label: (d)
Paragraph.Text: respecting the contents of the annual reports respecting the five-year plans;

Paragraph.Label: (e)
Paragraph.Text: respecting public participation in the development of five-year plans respecting climate change and climate change adaptation actions; and

Paragraph.Label: (f)
Paragraph.Text: imposing obligations on a port authority in respect of the climate change adaptation actions it must undertake.

Subsection.MarginalNote: Report to Parliament â reasons for absence of regulations
Subsection.Text: If no regulations are made for the purposes of each of paragraphs (1.1)(a) to (f) for an existing port authority within 36 months after the day on which this section comes into force or for a new port authority within 36 months after the day on which its letters patent are issued, the Minister must cause a report stating the reasons that no regulations have been made and establishing a schedule for making regulations to be laid before each House of Parliament on any of the first 10 days on which that House is sitting after the expiry of that 36-month period.

Subsection.MarginalNote: Absence of regulations after tabling of report
Subsection.Text: If no regulations are made within 12 months after the tabling of the report referred to in subsection (1.2), the Minister must cause a report stating the reasons that no regulations have been made and establishing a schedule for making regulations to be laid before each House of Parliament on any of the first 10 days on which that House is sitting after the expiry of that 12-month period and at least once every subsequent 12-month period as long as no regulations have been made.
A regulation made under subsection (1) or (1.1) may apply to only one port authority or wholly-owned subsidiary of a port authority.
A regulation made under subsection (1) or (1.1) may be made binding on His Majesty in right of Canada or a province.
Paragraph 28(2)(a) of the Act is replaced by the following:

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: port activities related to shipping, navigation, transportation of passengers and goods, handling of goods and storage of goods, including activities carried on in relation to real property and immovables that are not adjacent to navigable waters, to the extent that those activities are specified in the letters patent; and

Subsection.Text: Section 28 of the Act is amended by adding the following after subsection (2):

To the extent authorized in each participating port authority's letters patent, two or more port authorities may jointly, through a corporation, partnership, joint venture, association or other entity whose shares or other ownership interests are all held by port authorities, engage in the activities referred to in subsection (2).
Subsection 28(5.1) of the Act is repealed.

Section.Text: Section 30.1 of the Act is replaced by the following:

The directors of a port authority must submit its borrowing plan to the Minister within one year after the later of the day on which this section comes into force and the day on which its letters patent are issued and, after that, in accordance with the schedule set out in its letters patent.
The Act is amended by adding the following after section 33:

Subsection.Text: A port authority must establish one or more advisory committees, which must include representatives from the local community, local government and local Indigenous communities, in accordance with its letters patent.
The members of each advisory committee shall elect a chairperson from among their number.
A port authority must consult its advisory committees at least twice every year with respect to issues related to port activities.
A port authority must publish a summary of the consultations under subsection (2) on its website.

Section.Text: At least once every three years, a port authority must cause to be conducted an independent assessment of its governance practices in accordance with the regulations and submit a report to the Minister.
Subsection 35(2) of the Act is replaced by the following:

Subsection.Text: A port authority must, at least thirty days before the annual meeting, publish a notice on its website and have a notice published in a major newspaper published or distributed in the municipalities where the port is situated setting out the time and location of the meeting and specifying that the port authorityâs financial statements are available to the public on its website and at its registered office.
Subsection 37(1) of the Act is replaced by the following:

A port authority must, at least thirty days before the annual meeting, publish its audited annual financial statements and those of its wholly-owned subsidiaries for the preceding fiscal year on its website and make them available for inspection by the public at its registered office during normal business hours.
The portion of subsection 37(2) of the Act before paragraph (a) is replaced by the following:

The financial statements must be prepared in accordance with the International Financial Reporting Standards, adopted by the Accounting Standards Board and effective as of January 1, 2011, and consist of at least the following:

Section.Text: The Act is amended by adding the following after section 37:

Subsection.Text: Each port authority must, in respect of itself and its wholly-owned subsidiaries, cause to be prepared a quarterly financial report for each of the first three quarters of each financial year of the port authority.
The report must be prepared in accordance with the International Financial Reporting Standards, adopted by the Accounting Standards Board and effective as of January 1, 2011, and contain

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: a financial statement for the quarter and for the period from the start of the financial year to the end of that quarter;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: comparative financial information for the preceding financial year; and

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: a statement outlining the results, risks and significant changes in relation to operations, personnel and programs.

Subsection.Text: The port authority must publish the report on the port authorityâs website:

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: in the case of a port authority whose annual operating revenue is less than $20,000,000, within 90 days after the end of the quarter to which the report relates;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: in the case of any other port authority, within 60 days after the end of the quarter to which the report relates.

Subsection.Text: In the case of a port authority specified in paragraph 37.1(3)(a), the quarterly financial report must be prepared within a year after the day on which this section comes into force.

Subsection.MarginalNote: Publication â first three quarterly reports
Subsection.Text: Despite subsection (3), a port authority must publish on the port authorityâs website the reports for each of the first three quarters of its first financial year after its letters patent are issued within one year after the day on which its letters patent are issued.

Section.Text: Sections 39 and 40 of the Act are replaced by the following:

A port authority must, within three months after the end of each fiscal year, submit to the Minister, in respect of itself and each of its wholly-owned subsidiaries, a five-year business plan containing any information prescribed by regulation and a description of any material changes in respect of information provided in the previous business plan.

A port authorityâs financial statements, quarterly financial reports and business plan must be prepared in a form that clearly sets out information about each of the activities of the port authority and its wholly-owned subsidiaries, with the information about port activities referred to in paragraph 28(2)(a) set out separately from the information about other activities referred to in paragraph 28(2)(b).
Subsections 42(3) and (4) of the Act are replaced by the following:

Subsection.Text: A port authority must, as soon as is practicable after it receives the summary report, publish a notice of the summary report on its website and have that notice published in a major newspaper published or distributed in the municipalities where the port is situated.
A port authority must publish the summary report on its website and make it available for inspection by the public at its registered office during normal business hours.

Section.Text: The Act is amended by adding the following after section 43:

Subsection.Text: A port authority must prepare, in respect of itself and each of its wholly-owned subsidiaries, a five-year climate change plan within one year after the later of the day on which this section comes into force and the day on which its letters patent are issued and, after that, within five years after the previous plan was prepared.
The plan must contain

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: a greenhouse gas emissions reduction target in respect of the operation of a port by a port authority;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: a description of actions to be taken to achieve the greenhouse gas emissions reduction target;

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: information relating to any material changes in respect of information that was provided in the previous plan; and

Paragraph.Label: (d)
Paragraph.Text: any prescribed information.

Subsection.Text: A port authority must prepare, in respect of itself and each of its wholly-owned subsidiaries, a five-year plan respecting climate change adaptation actions within two years after the later of the day on which this section comes into force and the day on which its letters patent are issued and, after that, within five years after the previous plan was prepared.
The plan must contain

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: a description of the current and anticipated impacts of climate change on the operation of the port and on the assets managed by the port authority and any adaptation actions to be taken to address those impacts;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: a description of current and future commercial opportunities for the port authority arising from the impacts of climate change on the operation of the port and on the assets managed by the port authority, if any, and any actions to be taken to take advantage of them;

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: information relating to any material changes in respect of information that was provided in the previous plan; and

Paragraph.Label: (d)
Paragraph.Text: any prescribed information.

Section.Text: Each five-year plan must be developed in a manner consistent with recognized international standards.
The port authority must publish each five-year plan on its website no later than three months after the end of the fiscal year in which the plan was prepared.

Subsection.Text: A port authority must report annually on its five-year plans and must publish the report on its website within three months after the end of each fiscal year.
The report must contain

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: a greenhouse gas emissions inventory, set out in a manner consistent with recognized international standards, for emissions resulting from the operation of the port by the port authority;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: an update on the implementation of each five-year plan; and

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: any prescribed information.

Section.Text: Subsection 45(3.1) of the Act is replaced by the following:

Subsection.MarginalNote: Benefit of accession â leases in Quebec
Subsection.Text: A port authority situated in Quebec may, for the duration of a lease, renounce the benefit of accession in respect of any construction or work built on a federal immovable that the port authority manages.

The port authority may exercise the powers under subsections (3) and (3.01) to the same extent as His Majesty could exercise those powers and may, instead of His Majesty, execute and deliver the documents required for that purpose.
Subsection 48(1) of the Act is replaced by the following:

A port authority must, within 12 months after the issuance of its letters patent and after that in accordance with the schedule set out in its letters patent, develop a detailed land-use plan that contains objectives and policies for the physical development of the real property and immovables that it manages, holds or occupies and that takes into account relevant social, economic and environmental matters and zoning by-laws that apply to neighbouring lands.
The portion of subsection 48(2) of the English version of the Act before paragraph (a) is replaced by the following:
A land-use plan may
Subsections 48(4) to (8) of the Act are replaced by the following:
A port authority must, at least 60 days before the coming into force of a land-use plan,

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: publish a notice of the proposed land-use plan on its website and have that notice published in a major newspaper published or distributed in the municipalities where the port is situated; and

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: publish the proposed land-use plan and any related documents necessary to understand it on its website.

Subsection.Text: A notice of a proposed land-use plan must include information as to where a copy of the plan, including any related documents necessary to understand it, may be obtained and an invitation to any interested person to make representations to the port authority with respect to the proposed plan within those sixty days and to attend a public meeting at a specified time and place.

After the port authority considers any representations made by interested persons with respect to a proposed land-use plan and makes any amendments it considers appropriate as a result of those representations, it may adopt the plan.

Subsection.MarginalNote: Notice and publication of adopted plan
Subsection.Text: The port authority must

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: publish a notice of the adoption of the land-use plan that includes information as to where a copy of the plan may be obtained, on its website and have that notice published in a major newspaper published or distributed in the municipalities where the port is situated;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: publish a summary of the representations it received under subsection (5) on its website; and

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: publish the land-use plan on its website.

Section.Text: The portion of subsection 49(1) of the Act before paragraph (a) is replaced by the following:

Subsection.Text: A port authority may, subject to any regulations made under section 62, fix fees to be paid in respect of
Subsection 62(1) of the Act is amended by adding the following after paragraph (a):

Paragraph.Label: (a.1)
Paragraph.Text: the management of marine traffic, including mooring and anchorage, and the fees to be paid and the sharing of information and data by users and port authorities in support of that management;

Paragraph.Label: (a.2)
Paragraph.Text: the regulation of activities carried out by or on a vessel moored or at anchor in a port;

Subsection.Text: Subsection 62(1) of the Act is amended by adding the following after paragraph (d.1):

Paragraph.Label: (d.2)
Paragraph.Text: the information or documents that a port authority, a wholly-owned subsidiary of a port authority, an owner or a person in charge of a port facility or users must provide to the Minister;

Subsection.Text: Section 62 of the Act is amended by adding the following after subsection (1):
The Governor in Council may make regulations respecting the prohibition of the loading and unloading of thermal coal to and from ships in a port.

In making regulations under subsection (1.1), the Governor in Council must consult with relevant trade unions on ways to protect port workers who will be affected by the regulations, including by providing for continued collective bargaining, for the respect of collective agreements, for the creation of alternative opportunities in the marine transportation sector and for pension bridging.
Regulations made under subsection (1.1) must provide for the prohibition of the loading and unloading of all thermal coal to and from ships in a port no later than December 31, 2030.

Subsection.MarginalNote: Report to Parliament if no regulations under subsection (1.1)
Subsection.Text: If no regulations are made under subsection (1.1) within 48 months after the day on which this section comes into force, the Minister must cause a report stating the reasons that no regulations have been made and establishing a schedule for making regulations to be laid before each House of Parliament on any of the first 10 days on which that House is sitting after the expiry of that 48-month period.
Section 62 of the Act is amended by adding the following after subsection (1):
The Governor in Council may under paragraph (1)(b) make regulations respecting the prohibition of the deposit of raw sewage in waters under the jurisdiction of a port authority.

Section.Text: The portion of subsection 67(1) of the Act before paragraph (a) is replaced by the following:

Subsection.Text: The Minister may, by order, fix the fees to be paid in respect of

Section.Text: The Act is amended by adding the following after section 107:

Subsection.Text: If the Minister is of the opinion that there is a risk of imminent harm to national security, national economic security or competition that constitutes a significant threat to the safety and security of persons, goods, ships or port facilities or the security of supply chains, the Minister may, by order, require a port authority or a person in charge of a port facility to take any measure, including corrective measures, or stop any activity that the Minister considers necessary to prevent that harm.

The power under subsection (1) to make an order, and any powers, duties or functions conferred or imposed by or under such an order, must not be exercised or performed for the purpose of terminating a strike or lock-out or imposing a settlement in a labour dispute.
Every port authority and person in charge of a port facility subject to an order must comply with it once they have been notified of its substance.
The order is exempt from the application of sections 5, 6, 9, 11, 14, 17, 18 and 19 of the Statutory Instruments Act.
The Minister must publish the order on the Department of Transportâs website, and by any other means that the Minister considers appropriate.

Subsection (4) does not apply in respect of an order that, in the opinion of the Minister, contains confidential or privileged information or information the publication of which would pose a risk to national security, national economic security or competition.

Heading.TitleText: Consequential Amendment to the Transportation Appeal Tribunal of Canada Act

Section.Text: Subsection 2(3) of the Transportation Appeal Tribunal of Canada Act is replaced by the following:

Subsection.MarginalNote: Jurisdiction in respect of other Acts
Subsection.Text: The Tribunal also has jurisdiction in respect of reviews and appeals in connection with administrative monetary penalties provided for under sections 177 to 181 of the Canada Transportation Act, sections 43 to 55 of the International Bridges and Tunnels Act, sections 129.01 to 129.17 of the Canada Marine Act, sections 16.1 to 16.25 of the Motor Vehicle Safety Act, sections 39.1 to 39.26 of the Canadian Navigable Waters Act, sections 130.01 to 130.19 of the Marine Liability Act and sections 32.1 to 32.28 of the Transportation of Dangerous Goods Act, 1992.

Section.Text: If Bill C-26, introduced in the 1st session of the 44th Parliament and entitled An Act respecting cyber security, amending the Telecommunications Act and making consequential amendments to other Acts, receives royal assent, then, on the first day on which both section 18 of that Act and section 123 of this Act are in force, subsection 2(3) of the Transportation Appeal Tribunal of Canada Act is replaced by the following:

Subsection.MarginalNote: Jurisdiction in respect of other Acts
Subsection.Text: The Tribunal also has jurisdiction in respect of reviews and appeals in connection with administrative monetary penalties provided for under sections 177 to 181 of the Canada Transportation Act, sections 127 to 133 of the Critical Cyber Systems Protection Act, sections 43 to 55 of the International Bridges and Tunnels Act, sections 129.01 to 129.17 of the Canada Marine Act, sections 16.1 to 16.25 of the Motor Vehicle Safety Act, sections 39.1 to 39.26 of the Canadian Navigable Waters Act, sections 130.01 to 130.19 of the Marine Liability Act and sections 32.1 to 32.28 of the Transportation of Dangerous Goods Act, 1992.
Subsection 26(1) comes into force on a day to be fixed by order of the Governor in Council.
Sections 28 and 32 and subsection 42(3) come into force on a day to be fixed by order of the Governor in Council.
Sections 30 and 33 and subsection 42(2) come into force on a day to be fixed by order of the Governor in Council.
Section 40 and subsections 42(5) and 43(2) come into force on a day to be fixed by order of the Governor in Council.
Sections 94 to 98 come into force on a day to be fixed by order of the Governor in Council.
Section 99 comes into force on a day to be fixed by order of the Governor in Council.
Section 121 comes into force on a day to be fixed by order of the Governor in Council.
